title: Geospatial Distribution Dynamics With griddy
text: Pooled quantile classification cuts entire panel five bins reuses breakpoints every year. keeps class meanings comparable across periods, matters year-specific quantiles can hide absolute movement.
code: classes <- classify_dynamics(usjoin, name, year, income, k = 5)  class_intervals(classes) ## # A tibble: 5 × 4 ##   class  lower  upper type  ##   <ord>  <dbl>  <dbl> <chr> ## 1 Q1      127   1049. value ## 2 Q2     1049.  2238. value ## 3 Q3     2238.  7050. value ## 4 Q4     7050. 20739. value ## 5 Q5    20739. 54528  value

title: Geospatial Distribution Dynamics With griddy
text: diagonal dominance matrix — states stay starting quintile one year next — headline finding panel recovers result Rey (2001) reports.

title: Geospatial Distribution Dynamics With griddy
text: Spatial Markov conditions transition probabilities starting class unit’s spatial lag. expectation surrounded rich (poor) neighbors shifts transition odds beyond unit’s class predict. listw argument canonical way pass spatial weights — using prebuilt row-standardized object makes row-standardization choice explicit. spatial_markov() also accepts nb convenience converts internally spdep::nb2listw(style = "W").  Reading facets left right: states whose neighbors sit lowest income lag class (Q1) much stickier bottom distribution pooled transition matrix suggest. spatial context conditioning spatial Markov built surface.
code: states <- us_states |>   st_drop_geometry() |>   filter(NAME %in% usjoin$name) |>   pull(NAME)  geom <- us_states |>   filter(NAME %in% usjoin$name) |>   arrange(NAME)  panel <- usjoin |>   filter(name %in% states) |>   arrange(name, year)  listw <- nb2listw(poly2nb(geom, queen = TRUE), style = "W") spatial <- spatial_markov(panel, name, year, income, listw = listw, k = 5)  lag_intervals(spatial) ## # A tibble: 5 × 4 ##   class  lower  upper type        ##   <ord>  <dbl>  <dbl> <chr>       ## 1 Q1      181   1077. spatial_lag ## 2 Q2     1077.  2213. spatial_lag ## 3 Q3     2213.  7052. spatial_lag ## 4 Q4     7052. 21042. spatial_lag ## 5 Q5    21042. 52568. spatial_lag transition_matrix(spatial, "probability", lag_class = "Q1") ##            Q1        Q2 Q3 Q4 Q5 ## Q1 0.95270270 0.0472973  0  0  0 ## Q2 0.05263158 0.9473684  0  0  0 ## Q3         NA        NA NA NA NA ## Q4         NA        NA NA NA NA ## Q5         NA        NA NA NA NA plot_spatial_markov(spatial)

title: Geospatial Distribution Dynamics With griddy
text: Rank mobility intentionally simple v0.1: endpoint comparison unit ranks first last observed periods. descriptive map-ready, full PySAL rank-method suite.
code: mobility_panel <- usjoin |>   filter(name %in% states) |>   left_join(geom |> select(NAME), by = c("name" = "NAME")) |>   st_as_sf()  mobility <- rank_mobility(mobility_panel, name, year, income)  mobility |>   st_drop_geometry() |>   arrange(desc(abs_rank_change)) |>   select(name, start_rank, end_rank, rank_change) |>   head() ## <grd_rank_mobility> ## # A tibble: 6 × 4 ##   name         start_rank end_rank rank_change ##   <chr>             <int>    <int>       <int> ## 1 Virginia             36        6          30 ## 2 North Dakota         41       17          24 ## 3 Michigan             10       32         -22 ## 4 Ohio                 12       31         -19 ## 5 Utah                 29       47         -18 ## 6 Arizona              24       41         -17 plot_rank_mobility(mobility)

title: Geospatial Distribution Dynamics With griddy
text: result object exposes tidy transitions table long matrix table keyed class labels. Inspecting directly simplest way learn schema. programmatic access, every griddy object also .data.frame() S3 generics transition_matrix(), class_intervals(), (spatial objects) lag_intervals().
code: classic$transitions |> head() ## # A tibble: 6 × 6 ##   id      from_time to_time from_state to_state transition ##   <chr>       <int>   <int> <ord>      <ord>    <chr>      ## 1 Alabama      1929    1930 Q1         Q1       Q1 -> Q1   ## 2 Alabama      1930    1931 Q1         Q1       Q1 -> Q1   ## 3 Alabama      1931    1932 Q1         Q1       Q1 -> Q1   ## 4 Alabama      1932    1933 Q1         Q1       Q1 -> Q1   ## 5 Alabama      1933    1934 Q1         Q1       Q1 -> Q1   ## 6 Alabama      1934    1935 Q1         Q1       Q1 -> Q1 spatial$transitions |> select(id, from_time, to_time, lag_class, transition, spatial_lag) |> head() ## # A tibble: 6 × 6 ##   id      from_time to_time lag_class transition spatial_lag ##   <chr>       <int>   <int> <ord>     <chr>            <dbl> ## 1 Alabama      1929    1930 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          382. ## 2 Alabama      1930    1931 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          326  ## 3 Alabama      1931    1932 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          276. ## 4 Alabama      1932    1933 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          211  ## 5 Alabama      1933    1934 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          207. ## 6 Alabama      1934    1935 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          253.

title: Performance Benchmarks
text: 60 60 queen-contiguity grid ten time periods exercises classification spatial-lag code paths tract-scale panel .
code: make_panel <- function(nx = 60, ny = 60, years = 2010:2019) {   cells <- st_make_grid(st_bbox(c(xmin = 0, ymin = 0, xmax = nx, ymax = ny)), n = c(nx, ny))   grid <- st_sf(id = seq_along(cells), geometry = cells)    panel <- tidyr::crossing(id = grid$id, year = years) |>     left_join(st_drop_geometry(grid), by = "id") |>     mutate(value = id + as.integer(factor(year)) + rnorm(n())) |>     left_join(select(grid, id, geometry), by = "id") |>     st_as_sf()    list(grid = grid, panel = panel) }  fx <- make_panel() listw <- nb2listw(cell2nb(60, 60, type = "queen"), style = "W")  microbenchmark(   classify = classify_dynamics(fx$panel, id, year, value, k = 5),   markov = {     cls <- classify_dynamics(fx$panel, id, year, value, k = 5)     markov_dynamics(cls, id, year, class)   },   spatial = spatial_markov(fx$panel, id, year, value, listw = listw, k = 5),   times = 5 ) ## Warning in microbenchmark(classify = classify_dynamics(fx$panel, id, year, : ## less accurate nanosecond times to avoid potential integer overflows ## Unit: milliseconds ##      expr       min        lq      mean    median       uq       max neval cld ##  classify  15.76926  16.00082  16.57671  16.09894  17.0642  17.95033     5 a   ##    markov 121.17763 122.73038 125.70394 127.48233 128.4433 128.68604     5  b  ##   spatial 148.55555 152.64501 164.29052 153.36657 158.7998 208.08570     5   c

title: Performance Benchmarks
text: next chunk runs estdaR installed. calls griddy::spatial_markov() estdaR::sp.mkv() wide panel, matched class breaks, identical weights. difference point reflects implementation overhead, differing inputs. griddy carries long-format reshape, classification, label preservation overhead estdaR::sp.mkv() skips working directly unit--period matrix. expected pattern griddy slower panel size; much slower relevant question. gap exceeds order magnitude tract-scale panels revisited.
code: library(estdaR) ## Loading required package: ggplot2 ## Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.5.2 ##  ## Attaching package: 'estdaR' ## The following objects are masked from 'package:spdep': ##  ##     geary, moran wide_panel <- fx$panel |>   st_drop_geometry() |>   pivot_wider(id_cols = id, names_from = year, values_from = value) |>   arrange(id)  wide_mat <- as.matrix(wide_panel |> select(-id))  microbenchmark(   griddy = spatial_markov(fx$panel, id, year, value, listw = listw, k = 5),   estdaR = estdaR::sp.mkv(wide_mat, listw, classes = 5, fixed = TRUE),   times = 5 ) ## Unit: milliseconds ##    expr       min        lq      mean    median        uq       max neval cld ##  griddy 151.90914 154.17267 160.85877 161.11069 162.83433 174.26701     5  a  ##  estdaR  29.64776  30.68698  34.22886  33.58646  34.52204  42.70105     5   b

title: Comparing Against estdaR and spdyn
text: bundled usjoin panel mirrors PySAL’s reference dataset, prior R implementations griddy working series.
code: data(usjoin)  geom <- us_states |>   filter(NAME %in% usjoin$name) |>   arrange(NAME)  panel <- usjoin |>   filter(name %in% geom$NAME) |>   arrange(name, year)  listw <- nb2listw(poly2nb(geom, queen = TRUE), style = "W")  spatial <- spatial_markov(panel, name, year, income, listw = listw, k = 5)  spatial$transitions |>   select(id, from_time, to_time, lag_class, transition, spatial_lag) |>   head() ## # A tibble: 6 × 6 ##   id      from_time to_time lag_class transition spatial_lag ##   <chr>       <int>   <int> <ord>     <chr>            <dbl> ## 1 Alabama      1929    1930 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          382. ## 2 Alabama      1930    1931 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          326  ## 3 Alabama      1931    1932 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          276. ## 4 Alabama      1932    1933 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          211  ## 5 Alabama      1933    1934 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          207. ## 6 Alabama      1934    1935 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          253. lag_intervals(spatial) ## # A tibble: 5 × 4 ##   class  lower  upper type        ##   <ord>  <dbl>  <dbl> <chr>       ## 1 Q1      181   1077. spatial_lag ## 2 Q2     1077.  2213. spatial_lag ## 3 Q3     2213.  7052. spatial_lag ## 4 Q4     7052. 21042. spatial_lag ## 5 Q5    21042. 52568. spatial_lag transition_matrix(spatial, "count", lag_class = "Q1") ##     Q1 Q2 Q3 Q4 Q5 ## Q1 705 35  0  0  0 ## Q2   2 36  0  0  0 ## Q3   0  0  0  0  0 ## Q4   0  0  0  0  0 ## Q5   0  0  0  0  0

title: Comparing Against estdaR and spdyn
text: next chunk runs estdaR spdyn installed. package test suite carries static fixtures ordinary CI can validate spatial Markov counts without depending either package. two prior implementations griddy agree within numerical tolerance, three calls return TRUE. Disagreements treated bugs griddy. Disagreements one documented “Conventions worth knowing” .
code: library(estdaR) library(spdyn)  wide <- panel |>   pivot_wider(id_cols = name, names_from = year, values_from = income) |>   arrange(name)  wide_mat <- as.matrix(wide |> select(-name)) year_cols <- as.character(unique(panel$year))  estdar_sp <- estdaR::sp.mkv(wide_mat, listw, classes = 5, fixed = TRUE) spdyn_sp <- spdyn::spMarkov(   wide,   listw,   stateVars = year_cols,   n.states = 5,   pool = TRUE,   std = FALSE )  griddy_counts <- aperm(   array(     spatial$matrix$n,     dim = c(5, 5, 5),     dimnames = list(spatial$states, spatial$states, spatial$lag_states)   ),   c(2, 1, 3) )  all.equal(unname(griddy_counts), unname(estdar_sp$Transitions)) all.equal(unname(griddy_counts), unname(spdyn_sp$t))

title: Comparing Against estdaR and spdyn
text: Three places naive cross-comparison can produce false disagreement: Classification breaks. griddy uses pooled quantiles default; estdaR accepts fixed = TRUE/FALSE choose; spdyn exposes pool. Match comparing matrices. Standardization. spdyn::spMarkov(..., std = TRUE) standardizes panel year mean classifying. griddy standardize default. Set std = FALSE direct comparison. Output shape. estdaR returns 3D array indexed [, , lag]. spdyn returns object $t field shape. griddy returns long matrix tibble keyed (lag_class, from_state, to_state), makes downstream dplyr work natural. package test suite uses static fixtures estdaR::sp.mkv() spdyn::spMarkov() ordinary CI can validate spatial Markov counts without installing either package. Live cross-checks run conditionally via skip_if_not_installed().

title: griddy
text: griddy experimental R package geospatial distribution dynamics sf tidy data. inspired PySAL giddy established spatial distribution dynamics work, especially Rey (2001). methods-novelty project. Prior R implementations exist, including estdaR spdyn. package target maintained, CRAN-grade, long-data, map-ready workflow. Current scope: classify longitudinal spatial values comparable classes estimate classic Markov transition matrices estimate spatial Markov transition matrices conditioned spatial lag class compute simple rank mobility return tidy tables ggplot2 plots API scope still provisional.

title: griddy
text: minimal example also uses spData state geometry:
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("dshkol/griddy") install.packages("spData")

title: griddy
text: griddy keeps workflow keyed explicit id, time, value columns instead matrix row position. analytical outputs preserve transition labels, class intervals, spatial-lag intervals can inspected, joined, plotted without reverse-engineering array dimensions.

title: griddy
text: bundled usjoin panel canonical PySAL giddy reference dataset: 48 contiguous US states, per-capita personal income, 1929 2009. spatial_markov() accepts either listw (prebuilt spdep weights object) nb (neighbor list, converted internally via spdep::nb2listw(style = "W")). listw preferred row-standardization choice matters.
code: library(griddy) library(dplyr) library(sf) library(spData) library(spdep)  data(usjoin)  geom <- us_states |>   filter(NAME %in% usjoin$name) |>   arrange(NAME)  panel <- usjoin |>   filter(name %in% geom$NAME) |>   arrange(name, year)  listw <- nb2listw(poly2nb(geom, queen = TRUE), style = "W")  classes <- classify_dynamics(panel, name, year, income, k = 5) classic <- markov_dynamics(classes, name, year, class) spatial <- spatial_markov(panel, name, year, income, listw = listw, k = 5)  classic$transitions |> select(id, from_time, to_time, transition) |> head() lag_intervals(spatial)

what: Classify Longitudinal Values For Distribution Dynamics — classify_dynamics
title: Classify Longitudinal Values For Distribution Dynamics — classify_dynamics
text: Converts numeric values long panel ordered classes suitable transition analysis.

title: Classify Longitudinal Values For Distribution Dynamics — classify_dynamics
code: classify_dynamics(   data,   id,   time,   value,   method = c("pooled_quantile", "time_quantile", "fixed", "existing"),   k = 5,   breaks = NULL,   labels = NULL )

title: Classify Longitudinal Values For Distribution Dynamics — classify_dynamics
text: data data frame sf object long format. id, time, value Columns identifying spatial unit, time, value. method Classification method. "pooled_quantile" uses one set quantile breaks across periods. "time_quantile" uses period-specific ranks. "fixed" uses user-supplied breaks. "existing" treats value already classified state. k Number quantile classes. breaks Breaks method = "fixed". labels Optional class labels.

title: Classify Longitudinal Values For Distribution Dynamics — classify_dynamics
text: data frame sf object class column class grd_classes.

title: Classify Longitudinal Values For Distribution Dynamics — classify_dynamics
code: panel <- data.frame(   id = rep(letters[1:4], each = 3),   year = rep(2020:2022, times = 4),   value = c(8, 9, 11, 10, 12, 13, 15, 14, 16, 20, 22, 25) )  classes <- classify_dynamics(panel, id, year, value, k = 3) classes #> <grd_classes> #>    id year value class #> 1   a 2020     8    Q1 #> 2   a 2021     9    Q1 #> 3   a 2022    11    Q1 #> 4   b 2020    10    Q1 #> 5   b 2021    12    Q2 #> 6   b 2022    13    Q2 #> 7   c 2020    15    Q2 #> 8   c 2021    14    Q2 #> 9   c 2022    16    Q3 #> 10  d 2020    20    Q3 #> 11  d 2021    22    Q3 #> 12  d 2022    25    Q3 class_intervals(classes) #> # A tibble: 3 × 4 #>   class lower upper type  #>   <ord> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> #> 1 Q1      8    11.7 value #> 2 Q2     11.7  15.3 value #> 3 Q3     15.3  25   value

title: Estimate Spatial Markov Transition Dynamics — spatial_markov
code: spatial_markov(   data,   id,   time,   value,   nb = NULL,   listw = NULL,   k = 5,   lag_k = k,   class_method = c("pooled_quantile", "time_quantile", "fixed"),   zero.policy = TRUE )

title: Estimate Spatial Markov Transition Dynamics — spatial_markov
text: data long data frame sf object. id, time, value Columns identifying spatial unit, time, value. nb spdep neighbor list, used listw NULL. converted spdep::nb2listw(style = "W"). listw row-standardized spdep weights (listw) object. Preferred standardization choice matters. k Number value classes. lag_k Number spatial-lag classes. class_method Value classification method passed classify_dynamics(). zero.policy Passed spdep lag/weight helpers.

title: Estimate Spatial Markov Transition Dynamics — spatial_markov
text: grd_spatial_markov object.

title: Estimate Spatial Markov Transition Dynamics — spatial_markov
code: panel <- data.frame(   id = rep(1:4, each = 3),   year = rep(2020:2022, times = 4),   value = c(1, 2, 3, 2, 3, 4, 4, 3, 5, 5, 6, 7) )  listw <- spdep::nb2listw(spdep::cell2nb(2, 2), style = "W") spatial <- spatial_markov(panel, id, year, value, listw = listw, k = 2)  spatial #> <grd_spatial_markov> #> 2 states, 2 lag states, 8 observed transitions #> # A tibble: 8 × 8 #>   lag_class from_state to_state transition     n probability lag_lower lag_upper #>   <ord>     <ord>      <ord>    <chr>      <int>       <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 Q1        Q1         Q1       Q1 -> Q1       3       1           3         3.5 #> 2 Q1        Q1         Q2       Q1 -> Q2       0       0           3         3.5 #> 3 Q1        Q2         Q1       Q2 -> Q1       1       0.333       3         3.5 #> 4 Q1        Q2         Q2       Q2 -> Q2       2       0.667       3         3.5 #> 5 Q2        Q1         Q1       Q1 -> Q1       0       0           3.5       5   #> 6 Q2        Q1         Q2       Q1 -> Q2       2       1           3.5       5   #> 7 Q2        Q2         Q1       Q2 -> Q1       0      NA           3.5       5   #> 8 Q2        Q2         Q2       Q2 -> Q2       0      NA           3.5       5   lag_intervals(spatial) #> # A tibble: 2 × 4 #>   class lower upper type        #>   <ord> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>       #> 1 Q1      3     3.5 spatial_lag #> 2 Q2      3.5   5   spatial_lag transition_matrix(spatial, "count", lag_class = "Q1") #>    Q1 Q2 #> Q1  3  0 #> Q2  1  2

code: prob <- matrix(c(0.8, 0.2, 0.3, 0.7), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE) steady_state(prob) #> [1] 0.6 0.4

title: Extract A Transition Matrix — transition_matrix
text: Extract Transition Matrix

title: Extract A Transition Matrix — transition_matrix
code: transition_matrix(x, type = c("probability", "count"), lag_class = NULL)

title: Extract A Transition Matrix — transition_matrix
text: x grd_markov grd_spatial_markov object. type "probability" "count". lag_class Optional lag class grd_spatial_markov.

title: Extract A Transition Matrix — transition_matrix
text: matrix.

title: Extract A Transition Matrix — transition_matrix
code: panel <- data.frame(   id = rep(letters[1:4], each = 3),   year = rep(2020:2022, times = 4),   value = c(8, 9, 11, 10, 12, 13, 15, 14, 16, 20, 22, 25) ) markov <- panel |>   classify_dynamics(id, year, value, k = 3) |>   markov_dynamics(id, year, class)  transition_matrix(markov, "count") #>      #>      Q1 Q2 Q3 #>   Q1  2  1  0 #>   Q2  0  2  1 #>   Q3  0  0  2

what: US State Per-Capita Personal Income, 1929-2009 — usjoin
title: US State Per-Capita Personal Income, 1929-2009 — usjoin
text: Per-capita personal income 48 contiguous US states nominal dollars. Mirrors canonical usjoin panel used PySAL giddy spatial Markov literature, examples validation package directly comparable reference work.

title: US State Per-Capita Personal Income, 1929-2009 — usjoin
code: usjoin

title: US State Per-Capita Personal Income, 1929-2009 — usjoin
text: tibble 3,888 rows (48 states x 81 years) 4 columns: name State name. state_fips State FIPS code, integer. year Year, integer 1929 2009. income Nominal per-capita personal income, integer USD.

title: US State Per-Capita Personal Income, 1929-2009 — usjoin
text: PySAL libpysal examples/us_income/usjoin.csv. original series constructed US Bureau Economic Analysis state personal income tables. See https://github.com/pysal/libpysal.

title: US State Per-Capita Personal Income, 1929-2009 — usjoin
text: Rey, S. J. (2001). Spatial empirics economic growth convergence. Geographical Analysis, 33(3), 195-214. Rey, S. J., Kang, W., & Wolf, L. (2016). properties tests spatial effects discrete Markov chain models regional income distribution dynamics. Journal Geographical Systems, 18(4), 377-398.

title: US State Per-Capita Personal Income, 1929-2009 — usjoin
code: data(usjoin) head(usjoin) #> # A tibble: 6 × 4 #>   name    state_fips  year income #>   <chr>        <int> <int>  <int> #> 1 Alabama          1  1929    323 #> 2 Alabama          1  1930    267 #> 3 Alabama          1  1931    224 #> 4 Alabama          1  1932    162 #> 5 Alabama          1  1933    166 #> 6 Alabama          1  1934    211 range(usjoin$year) #> [1] 1929 2009

title: griddy 0.0.0.9000 (development)
text: Initial development release.

title: griddy 0.0.0.9000 (development)
text: classify_dynamics(), markov_dynamics(), spatial_markov(), rank_mobility() accept long sf panels keyed explicit id, time, value columns. transition_matrix(), steady_state(), class_intervals(), lag_intervals() provide tidy access results. plot_transition_matrix(), plot_spatial_markov(), plot_rank_mobility() return ggplot2 objects.

title: griddy 0.0.0.9000 (development)
text: Bundled usjoin: 48 contiguous US state per-capita personal income, 1929–2009, mirroring PySAL’s reference dataset spatial Markov examples.

title: griddy 0.0.0.9000 (development)
text: Static fixtures cross-checked estdaR::sp.mkv() spdyn::spMarkov(). Optional live cross-checks run packages PySAL giddy installed.
